name: compare-prose
description: Two prose options side-by-side with a labeled corner tag on each.
purpose: Use to weigh two approaches against each other in body text. Add the `chosen` or `decision` modifier to mark the verdict; add `vertical` to stack top/bottom instead of side-by-side.

options.description: Exactly two list items, each one option. Lead each with **Option label.** then 1–3 sentences.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: compare-prose -->

## Heading framing the comparison.

- **First option.** Two-sentence description of the first option, including the strongest argument for it.
- **Second option.** Two-sentence description of the second option, including the strongest argument for it.
sample:
<!-- _class: compare-prose -->

## Two options, equal weight, head-to-head.

- First option
  - Two-sentence description of the first option, including the strongest argument for it. Equal-density prose lets the audience compare line by line.
- Second option
  - Two-sentence description of the second option, including the strongest argument for it. The `chosen` or `decision` modifier marks the verdict when one has been made.

whenToUse.title: Two prose alternatives
whenToUse.body: Both sides are full sentences of argument, not lists of facts. The audience reads each column as a paragraph and weighs them against each other.

whenToUse.title: Equal-density prose
whenToUse.body: Each card carries roughly the same body length. One short and one long breaks the visual symmetry that makes the comparison legible.

whenToUse.title: Add a verdict modifier when chosen
whenToUse.body: Layer `chosen`, `decision`, or `vertical` to name the editorial intent. The default (neutral two-up) reads as still-being-decided.

antiPatterns.title: Code comparison
antiPatterns.body: Use `compare-code` for two fenced blocks. compare-prose is for sentences, not snippets.

antiPatterns.title: Three or more options
antiPatterns.body: compare-prose is strictly two. For three or more, use `cards-grid three` or `verdict-grid` with criteria badges.

antiPatterns.title: Verbatim text differences
antiPatterns.body: When the diff lives inside the prose itself — legal language, contract clauses — use `redline` so insertions and deletions render inline.

related.name: before-after
related.when: two states of one system, not two alternatives

related.name: compare-code
related.when: the columns are code, not prose

related.name: split-compare
related.when: the verdict needs a bottom recommendation bar

related.name: verdict-grid
related.when: three or more options scored against shared criteria

related.name: decision
related.when: the verdict slide that lands after a comparison

mirror.label: Mirror — swap left and right
mirror.caption: Flips the two cards left-to-right. Use when the deck's visual rhythm or the natural reading order wants the second option on the left.
mirror.sample:
<!-- _class: compare-prose mirror -->

## Same comparison, columns swapped.

- First option
  - Now rendered on the right, second in the reading order. Useful when the natural argument flow wants the alternative considered before the lead.
- Second option
  - Now rendered on the left. Pair with `chosen` to mark the swapped position as the verdict.

chosen.label: Chosen — second card is the winner
chosen.caption: Marks the right card as the verdict with an accent left edge and tinted background. The post-processor always emits left-then-right; put the considered option first and the choice second.
chosen.sample:
<!-- _class: compare-prose chosen -->

## The right card is the verdict.

- Build in-house
  - Full control of the schema and roadmap, but 2–3 engineer-quarters before feature parity. Maintenance burden stays internal.
- Buy + configure
  - Ships in 6 weeks, not 9 months. Engineering capacity redirects to product-layer features; exit risk is manageable via contractual data export.

decision.label: Decision — left rejected, right chosen, connector labelled
decision.caption: The full editorial composition: left card de-emphasised (struck title + muted body), right card emphasised, the connector amplified and labelled DECISION. The most common variant in real decks.
decision.sample:
<!-- _class: compare-prose decision -->

## Build vs buy — decided.

- Build in-house
  - Full control of the schema and roadmap, but 2–3 engineer-quarters before feature parity. Maintenance burden stays internal.
- Buy + configure
  - Ships in 6 weeks, not 9 months. Engineering capacity redirects to product-layer features; exit risk is manageable via contractual data export.

vertical.label: Vertical — stack cards top-to-bottom
vertical.caption: Stacks the two cards vertically and rotates the connector 90°. Use for long-body comparisons where the side-by-side format would crowd the prose.
vertical.sample:
<!-- _class: compare-prose vertical -->

## Long-body options stacked for room.

- Build in-house
  - Full control over the schema and the roadmap, with 2–3 engineer-quarters before feature parity. Ongoing maintenance burden stays internal; the team owns every escalation, every migration, every breaking change. Worth it when the data model is the differentiation; expensive when it isn't.
- Buy + configure
  - Ships in 6 weeks rather than 9 months, with engineering capacity redirecting to product-layer features the customer actually pays for. Exit risk is bounded by contractual data export; switching cost is a known number rather than a moving target. The right call when the data layer is plumbing rather than differentiation.
